title: The Tenth Sunday after Pentecost
context:
# TEMPORAL CONTEXT SNAPSHOT
Sermon: "The Tenth Sunday after Pentecost" | Saint John's Episcopal Cathedral, Denver
August 9, 2020 | Preacher: Broderick Greer

---

## 1. LITURGICAL CONTEXT

The Tenth Sunday after Pentecost falls within Ordinary Time (also called the Season after Pentecost), the longest liturgical season in the Christian calendar spanning from late May through late November. Under the Revised Common Lectionary, readings for this date would likely include Genesis 37:1-4, 12-28 (the Joseph narrative), Psalm 105:1-6, 16-22, 45b, Romans 10:5-15, and Matthew 14:22-33 (Jesus walking on water). The emphasis during this portion of Ordinary Time centers on faith, providence, and divine care amid uncertainty—themes that would resonate deeply given the concurrent national circumstances.

## 2. EPISCOPAL / ANGLICAN CHURCH NEWS

The Episcopal Church was in the midst of navigating pandemic worship guidelines with significant variation across dioceses; some congregations had resumed limited in-person services while others remained fully virtual. The Diocese of Colorado, under Bishop Kimberly Lucas (elected in 2018), was managing reopening protocols and developing spiritual resources for a fractured congregation. Nationally, the Church was processing the aftermath of the June 2020 General Convention resolution condemning racism and white supremacy, which had passed with unusual near-unanimity but left implementation questions about institutional change unresolved.

## 3. DENVER & COLORADO NEWS

Denver was experiencing significant civil unrest related to police brutality and racial justice protests that had intensified following George Floyd's death in May. The downtown Denver area, where Saint John's Cathedral is located, had been a focal point for demonstrations throughout July and early August, with clashes between protesters and police reported regularly. Colorado was also in the grip of severe wildfire season, with the Pine Gulch Fire (started July 30) becoming one of the largest fires in state history by early August, forcing evacuations and filling the region with smoke and air quality warnings.

## 4. NATIONAL NEWS (US)

The COVID-19 pandemic remained the dominant news story, with the US death toll approaching 165,000 and cases surging in southern and western states; school reopening decisions were intensely contested between federal pressure and public health concerns. The presidential campaign between incumbent Donald Trump and former Vice President Joe Biden was intensifying, with the Democratic National Convention held virtually August 17-20 (just after this sermon), reshaping political discourse. Civil unrest continued nationwide following months of protests over George Floyd's death and police brutality, with tensions between federal and local authorities over deployment of federal agents to cities like Portland, Oregon, creating constitutional questions about federal overreach.

## 5. INTERNATIONAL NEWS

The ongoing Syrian civil war saw continued humanitarian crisis with millions internally displaced and facing famine conditions as economic collapse deepened. India and Pakistan tensions remained elevated around the Kashmir region, with diplomatic protests and military posturing continuing. The global pandemic was accelerating in Latin America and parts of Africa, with Brazil, India, and Peru experiencing exponential case growth and healthcare system strain.

## 6. CULTURAL MOMENT

The national mood was deeply fractured and anxious—split between pandemic fatigue and fear, racial justice reckoning, and partisan polarization heading into the final three months before a contentious election. The phrase "unprecedented times" had become ubiquitous in both secular and religious discourse, reflecting genuine disorientation about normalcy and future. For a Denver congregation in August 2020, the combination of smoke-filled skies from wildfires, visible downtown protests, and isolation from pandemic restrictions created a particularly vivid sense of social fragmentation and environmental foreboding that would have weighed on pastoral messaging about faith and providence.
